story:
Tommy wakes to his mother’s Minnesota voice—her I’m-not-thrilled-with-you tone—warning he’ll be late. Ten is old enough, he figures, not to do what’s good for him. Outside, the heat is already rising, recalling yesterday’s soccer practice where, as goalie, he flopped between the posts because the team was at the other end and the sun was merciless.

Today is the class’s canyon hike, and there’s a catch: no uniform, no field trip. Tommy ransacks drawers and laundry piles, boxers perched on his head like a hat, but his blue polo and navy shorts have vanished. Mom, at DEFCON 4 silence, offers only, “Find an alternative.” With the van idling and her patience thinning, Tommy emerges in his school’s dress uniform—blazer with crest, white oxford, tie, navy wool pants. Not ideal for hiking, but technically a uniform.

At school, Ms. Forester blinks but lets it go. Meanwhile, Caitlyn—class president, fast runner, always composed—has forgotten her uniform. She raids the lost-and-found and ends up in a polo and shorts so big they threaten to puddle around her ankles. Maddie, quick-thinking and kind, loans a belt and uses a spare hair tie to knot the shirt. They slip onto the bus before Ms. Forester can notice.

At the nature preserve, the ranger reviews plants and safety. Tommy feels his wool pants turn into portable ovens. The bus has already left, so he knots his blazer around his waist and rolls up his sleeves, sweat trickling under his collar. Caitlyn, in billowy, borrowed gear, discovers oversized can be breezy.

By lunch, Maddie and Caitlyn have led the pack to the picnic area. From the heap of class lunches, Caitlyn jogs back with Tommy’s sack, and he staggers in dusty and disheveled, tie tip poking from his pocket, shirt untucked. He thanks her—then squints. He tugs at the tag sticking up from her collar. “Hey! That’s my shirt.” It is: TOMMY SPARKS. Suddenly, everything clicks. The lost-and-found. Of course.

Caitlyn shrugs. There were tons like it; the Mother’s Guild is hauling the box to the used-uniform sale next weekend. If Tommy wants his shirts, he’ll have to buy them back. Tommy’s brain runs a montage: soccer days when he swapped polos for jerseys and never swapped back; after-school basketball, always skins, shirt abandoned; changing for swimming and bolting straight to the pool, uniform left who-knows-where. Mystery solved, lesson pending.

By day’s end, Tommy’s dress uniform is a dust magnet. In the pickup line, Mom sees the carnage and powers straight to DEFCON 4—eyes only. Tommy grins anyway. “I think I found where all my shirts are.”

The next weekend, while Austin pedals by to invite him over, Tommy is raking and trimming hedges. Consequence from Mom: earn enough to buy back the shirts; maybe then he’ll learn to put things away. Austin nods and rides on. At dusk, Mom calls dinner. Tommy drops the rake into the leaf pile, peels off his sweaty T-shirt, tosses it onto the rake—and hesitates. He feels the tug of a thought: What am I forgetting? “Tommy! Dinner!” He shrugs and runs inside, leaving the shirt exactly where future-Tommy will have to find it. Or won’t.
